evals.prompt: Add Botocraft support for AWS Backup. Start with discovery, identify the service alias and one or two high-confidence primary models, then author the first CRUDL-capable slice using repository patterns instead of generic AWS guesses.
evals.expected_output: The skill should route through discovery first, keep first scope narrow, and produce Botocraft-specific model and manager decisions for a straightforward CRUDL service.

evals.prompt: Add support for a new AWS service whose resources are readonly and whose list APIs mostly return ARNs rather than full objects. I still want Botocraft list methods to return model instances. Add only the minimum handwritten shaping needed.
evals.expected_output: The skill should route to discovery and advanced manager guidance, recognize readonly contract, and choose decorator-first identifier-to-model conversion patterns.

evals.prompt: Author a new Botocraft service where several model names collide with existing services and one nested field's type would likely have the same name as the field itself. Walk carefully so sync survives the first pass.
evals.expected_output: The skill should route into advanced model guidance, use alternate_name or targeted field/type fixes, and treat collisions as first-class source-of-truth work.

evals.prompt: Build Botocraft support for an AWS API whose main user-facing resource does not map cleanly to one botocore shape and probably needs assembled state from several calls. Decide whether the primary model should be bespoke and explain why.
evals.expected_output: The skill should escalate to advanced model guidance, compare shape-backed versus bespoke options, and choose bespoke only when simpler YAML patterns are insufficient.

evals.prompt: Help me design a Botocraft service as complicated as EC2: multiple primary models, cross-service name collisions, tags that need conversion, decorator-shaped get/list methods, relations, and extra lifecycle helpers. Keep context lean and load the EC2-specific guidance only when needed.
evals.expected_output: The skill should keep parent routing thin, load reference material instead of bloating active instructions, and choose existing Botocraft EC2/ECS-style patterns rather than generic AWS advice.

evals.prompt: Add a Botocraft service where some resources do not have a clean global get, and list only works when you first supply a parent context such as a bucket or rule. I want the skill to model that honestly rather than forcing fake CRUDL symmetry.
evals.expected_output: The skill should recognize service gaps and exceptions, route to advanced manager guidance when needed, and preserve honest context-required manager methods.

evals.prompt: Help me add Botocraft support for an AWS service whose relation guesses will need AWS docs. First prefer aws-knowledge-mcp-server if it is available. If it is not installed, tell me that and ask whether I want to install it. If I say no, continue with official AWS docs on the web instead of stalling.
evals.expected_output: The skill should prefer aws-knowledge-mcp-server when available, explicitly ask about installation when it is missing, and proceed with official AWS web docs if the user declines installation.

evals.prompt: I want help adding relations for a new Botocraft service. Please inspect the primary models, suggest likely relations first, let me review and reject bad ones, and only then tell me what should go into `relations:`.
evals.expected_output: The skill should use a two-stage relation workflow, stop after candidate review in stage 1, and avoid jumping straight to YAML edits before user approval.

evals.prompt: Find likely Botocraft relations for a service where fields include its own ResourceArn and ResourceId, plus nested TargetGroupArns and a composite ResourceId that might point at another AWS resource. I only want relations that truly target primary models, and I want weak guesses left raw.
evals.expected_output: The skill should exclude self-identity fields, use doc-assisted fuzzy discovery for the composite identifier, suggest only primary-model relations, and keep low-confidence candidates unimplemented until approved.
